summary: A long-lived spring butterfly that overwinters as an adult, breeds on buckthorns, and remains common in Sweden even though the visible GBIF footprint is strongly amplified by observer-rich countries.

fieldRecord.habitatNote: The available images show an overwintered adult resting among dry leaf litter and woody vegetation in early-spring light.
fieldRecord.notes: Photo-backed profile seeded from GBIF, the Swedish Red List dataset, and DOI-checked literature. Add exact date, place, and coordinates if you want the page to show a field-record map.

notes: Taxonomy cross-checked against the GBIF Backbone entry used by the site.
This sheet does not claim subspecies-level treatment.

hero.headline: Brimstone
hero.strapline: One of the first butterflies to signal spring in Sweden, but ecologically more interesting than its familiarity suggests: it is long-lived, host-limited, and seasonally mobile.

observation.notes: Legacy compatibility block; exact field metadata has not been added yet for these images.

media.alt: Overwintered Gonepteryx rhamni resting closed-winged in dry leaf litter, showing the leaf-like underside and pointed forewing tip.
media.caption: Closed wings show why resting brimstones disappear so easily into last year’s leaves.

media.alt: Close handheld view of Gonepteryx rhamni showing the greenish underside, fine venation, and orange discal spot.
media.caption: Close view of the underside pattern and the small orange discal spot on the wing.

media.alt: Pale spring brimstone with closed wings held in the hand against a forest backdrop.
media.caption: The thin pale wing and leaf-tip shape remain visible even in a worn spring individual.

ecologyHighlights: Larvae are strongly tied to buckthorns, especially alder buckthorn (Frangula alnus) and common buckthorn (Rhamnus cathartica).
Unlike many butterflies, the species overwinters as an adult and can therefore appear very early in spring.
Adults use different seasonal habitat sets: breeding depends on host-plant availability, while summer persistence can favor cooler and more wooded sites.
The species is mobile enough to track patchy resources, but local breeding still remains constrained by larval host plants.
The sharply pointed wings and leaf-like underside provide effective camouflage when the butterfly rests with wings closed.

distribution.text: Gonepteryx rhamni is a widespread western-Palearctic brimstone, familiar across much of Europe and recorded far beyond Sweden. The live GBIF observation layer for this project is enormous, but it is dominated by high-reporting countries rather than reading as a direct abundance map.
distribution.caveat: GBIF density here mostly reflects where butterfly recording platforms are strong, where records have been digitized, and where observer effort is high. The UK and the Netherlands dominate the filtered layer, so map density should not be read as a simple biological abundance gradient.

global.overview: At broad scale this is a western-Palearctic species. The Europe-focused conservation sources used here support a widespread European interpretation; the farther Asian extent should be treated as background range context rather than a claim about continuous occupancy everywhere.
global.nativeRange: Native across much of Europe and extending eastward into the temperate Palearctic. For this page, the defensible core statement is that it is a widespread European brimstone rather than a narrow-range specialist.

map.title: Global GBIF occurrence records
map.subtitle: Human or machine observations from 2000 onwards, filtered to present records only.

sweden.overview: In Sweden the species is resident and reproducing, and the 2025 national assessment treats it as Livskraftig (LC). It is common enough to be familiar, but still useful as a teaching species for spring phenology, host-plant dependence, and observer bias in open biodiversity data.
sweden.focus: The Sweden-only GBIF layer is much thinner than the UK layer but still substantial, with more than 100,000 filtered records. Much of that density likely reflects where people report butterflies frequently, not only where the species is most biologically concentrated.

map.title: Sverige · högre upplösning
map.subtitle: Sweden-only GBIF records using the same filters as the global layer.

internationalContext.overview: Internationally, Gonepteryx rhamni is a good example of a butterfly that is both widespread and ecologically specific. It is not a rarity story; it is a seasonal-habitat and host-plant story.

status.system: European Red List of Butterflies / IUCN Europe
status.label: Least Concern
status.summary: The Europe-scale assessment treats the species as Least Concern rather than as a near-threshold conservation case.

ecology: Breeding distribution is closely linked to the availability of larval buckthorns, especially Frangula alnus and Rhamnus cathartica.
The species overwinters as an adult, making it unusually long-lived for a butterfly that remains conspicuous in both spring and late summer.
Field studies from Spain support seasonal elevational movement, with cooler and more wooded summer sites used differently from breeding sites.
Adults respond to floral scent cues and are not restricted to a single nectar source in the way larvae are restricted to host plants.
The wing underside works as strong leaf mimicry, while male wing colour is produced through pierid pigment and structural effects.
threats: Local breeding can be limited where larval host shrubs are scarce, fragmented, or removed from the landscape.
Landscape simplification can decouple breeding habitat from summer shelter and overwintering cover.
In mountain systems, climate change can tighten the spacing between seasonal habitat networks needed across the annual cycle.
visitorHooks: Check the first warm days of spring: this is one of the earliest butterflies many people notice after winter.
Compare the breeding-season host-plant story with the summer movement story; the same butterfly uses the landscape differently across the year.
Watch the GBIF map critically: it is a good case study in how observer effort shapes open biodiversity layers.

swedishContext.overview: In Sweden, citronfjäril is a classic spring signal rather than a red-list concern. That makes it useful on this site for a different reason: it links familiar field experience to real questions about host plants, phenology, overwintering strategy, and data bias.
swedishContext.rangeNote: The bundled Swedish Red List index treats the species as resident and reproducing in Sweden. The site should therefore frame Swedish records as part of a stable native presence, not as an occasional immigrant story.
habitat: Woodland edges, scrubby mosaics, hedgerows, and damp or semi-open places where buckthorns grow.
Woody cover matters not only for breeding landscapes but also for shelter and overwintering.
Adult observations can pile up in sunny spring corridors even when the larval host plants are tucked into wetter or shrubbier nearby patches.
swedishContext.phenology: Adults can appear very early in spring after overwintering, breed when host plants flush, and produce a summer generation that later seeks overwintering shelter.
specialFacts: Citronfjäril is one of the easiest Swedish butterflies to notice early in the year because the adult survives winter.
The 2025 Swedish Red List marks the species as Livskraftig (LC), not as a current national concern.
Its familiar status makes it useful for teaching that common species can still have narrow larval requirements and complex annual movements.

redList2025.documentation: Populationen är ökande. De skattade värdena som bedömningen baserar sig på ligger alla inom intervallet för kategorin Livskraftig (LC).

artfakta.note: Use Artfakta as the main Swedish species-information source when refreshing this page locally.

similarSpecies.name: Gonepteryx cleopatra
similarSpecies.note: The closest look-alike within the genus, but males show a stronger orange flush on the upperside and the species is not the standard Swedish brimstone.

similarSpecies.name: Pieris whites and Colias yellows
similarSpecies.note: These can match the pale-yellow impression at a distance, but they lack the sharply pointed leaf-mimic wing shape and the characteristic orange discal spots of a resting brimstone.

whyReturn: The page becomes more interesting every spring because first-flight timing shifts noticeably with weather.
It is a good species for comparing what a common butterfly looks like in the field versus what it looks like in open-data layers.
New host-plant or habitat notes would materially improve the Swedish context without needing a rare-species story.

selectedLiterature.title: Oviposition strategies in Pieridae butterflies and the role of an egg-killing plant trait therein
selectedLiterature.relevance: Useful for breeding ecology: it documents oviposition placement for G. rhamni on natural host plants.

selectedLiterature.title: Climate conditions and resource availability drive return elevational migrations in a single-brooded insect
selectedLiterature.relevance: Best source here for seasonal habitat partitioning, uphill summer movement, and climate-related constraints.

selectedLiterature.title: Colors and pterin pigmentation of pierid butterfly wings
selectedLiterature.relevance: Supports the colour-structure context for pierid wings, including the conspicuous wing appearance of brimstones.

selectedLiterature.title: Foraging responses in the butterflies Inachis io, Aglais urticae, and Gonepteryx rhamni to floral scents
selectedLiterature.relevance: Useful for adult feeding ecology because it shows behavioral responses to floral scent cues.

sourceFiles.note: This page-ready JSON draft was created from the local species-sheet schema plus targeted source checks, not from a full packet run in a networked Python environment.

buildNotes.compatibility: Netlify-ready static build. The build script scans species_sheets/ and img/, writes a manifest to dist/species_sheets/index.json, and deploys a static front end plus optional Netlify functions for live GBIF points.
buildNotes.recommendedLocalNextStep: If you want a fully materialized evidence packet beside this page, run the bundled species-sheet-builder scripts in a networked environment and attach the resulting taxonomy, occurrence, and literature artifacts.
